---
title: "The Normal (Gaussian) Distribution"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE  = "#2b3a67"     # the density curve / data
CURVE2 = "#c1121f"     # comparison curve / fitted line
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the mean
GREY   = "#6c757d"

def normal_pdf(x, mu=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    """The Gaussian density this article derives, used here only to draw
    the pictures: (1/sqrt(2 pi sigma^2)) exp(-(x-mu)^2 / (2 sigma^2))."""
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)
    z = (x - mu) / sigma
    return np.exp(-0.5 * z * z) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2.0 * np.pi))
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the fourth stop in the **distribution
> catalog** of the Probability Foundations track. It assumes [PROB-4 *Continuous Random
> Variables: the Probability Density Function*] for what a density *is* and how
> $P(a\le X\le b)=\int_a^b f$ recovers probabilities; [PROB-5 *Expectation*] and
> [PROB-6 *Variance*] for how we compute a mean and a variance from a density; and
> [PROB-8 *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*] for the rule that the joint density of
> independent variables factors. We touch exactly one tool above the Calculus-I floor —
> a single *double* integral evaluated in *polar coordinates* (the Gaussian-integral
> trick) — and we build it on the page rather than assume it, flagging it as it arrives.
> It **sets up** [PROB-15 *Chi-Square, Student's t, and F*], which are all manufactured
> out of normals; [PROB-17–PROB-18 *Random Vectors* and *Linear Transforms*], where the
> bell curve becomes the multivariate normal; and [PROB-20 *Moment Generating
> Functions*], which re-derives several facts proved here by hand. Downstream it is
> consumed almost everywhere: by [REGR-2] and [REGR-9] (the Gaussian error model and its
> inference) and across the Statistical Inference track.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The normal distribution is the function
$$
f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr),
$$
the bell curve. This article does three things and defends one claim. It **derives the
mysterious constant $1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}$ from scratch** — the only step above the
Calculus-I floor, the polar-coordinates evaluation of the Gaussian integral, built in
full. It shows that $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ really are the mean and variance, by computing
both integrals. And it proves the one structural property that makes the normal special
among all distributions: **an independent sum of normals is itself normal**, with means
and variances simply adding. The single claim the article defends is that the bell curve
is not important because it is common in nature — that story is mostly folklore — but
because it is the **fixed point of averaging**: the shape that sums and averages of many
independent things are dragged toward, and the shape that stays itself under the linear
operations statistics is built from. We close on the honest boundary: its tails are so
thin that trusting them on heavy-tailed data is one of the most expensive mistakes in
applied work.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The normal distribution $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ has density proportional to
$\exp\!\bigl(-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2\bigr)$ — a smooth hump centered at $\mu$, with width
set by $\sigma$. Two facts make it the most important distribution in statistics. First,
**it is closed under the operations we use most**: shift it, scale it, or add independent
copies of it, and you get another normal — the means add, the variances add, the *shape*
never changes. Second, by the Central Limit Theorem it is the **universal limit of
standardized sums** of independent pieces, so the moment you average data the bell curve
appears whether or not the data themselves were ever bell-shaped. Its one liability is
the same exponential that makes it tractable: its tails vanish so fast that it
catastrophically under-rates rare extreme events.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The intimidating constant out front,
$1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}$, will look like it was handed down from a mountain; by
@sec-gaussian-integral you will have *derived* the $\sqrt{2\pi}$ yourself, and it will be
the only thing it could have been. The closure property — sums of normals are normal —
will be proved in @sec-closure as the engine behind why estimators in every downstream
track end up normal.

# Why we are here: the curve that came out of *errors* {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **why this exact curve, and why does it
deserve its place at the center of statistics?** Every derivation below answers a piece of
it. If a step ever feels arbitrary, come back here.

## The problem it was invented to solve

The bell curve was not discovered by staring at heights or test scores. It came out of
the same eighteenth-century problem that produced least squares (the origin story of
[REGR-1]): astronomers and surveyors had **many noisy measurements of one quantity** —
the position of a comet, the length of a meridian arc — that refused to agree. They needed
a principled answer to two questions: how are these measurement *errors* distributed, and
given the disagreeing numbers, what is the best single estimate?

Abraham de Moivre stumbled on the shape first, around 1733, while doing something
completely different: approximating the binomial distribution for large $n$. If you flip a
fair coin $n$ times, the number of heads is $\mathrm{Binomial}(n,\tfrac12)$ (built in
[PROB-11]); de Moivre found that for large $n$ the staircase of binomial bars is hugged by
a smooth exponential curve. He had found the bell curve as a *limit of sums* — which, as
we will see in @sec-why-everywhere, is exactly why it matters — but he had no framework to
make it mean anything.

Carl Friedrich Gauss gave it that meaning in 1809. He asked the inverse question: *if* the
most natural estimate of a quantity from repeated measurements is their arithmetic
average, what must the error distribution be? He showed the answer is forced to be
$\exp(-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2)$ — the average is the most-likely value precisely when errors
follow this curve and no other. That is why it is called the *Gaussian*, and why it is
welded to the method of least squares: minimizing squared error *is* finding the
most-likely line under Gaussian noise, the equivalence we derived step by step in
@sec-why-gaussian of [REGR-1]. Pierre-Simon Laplace then connected it to averaging in
general, the seed of the Central Limit Theorem.

So the *why* is baked into the formula from birth. The curve exists to model the scatter
of independent errors around a true value, and its defining algebraic features — the
*squared* deviation in the exponent, the symmetry, the single peak at $\mu$ — are the
fingerprints of that origin. We will see each of those features fall out with a reason
attached, never by decree.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the normal distribution common because nature loves bell curves?"* No, and saying so
is the mark of someone who has not thought it through. Plenty of natural quantities are
*not* normal — incomes, city sizes, file sizes, and financial returns are famously heavy-
tailed; waiting times are skewed (the exponential and gamma of [PROB-14]); counts are
discrete (Poisson, [PROB-12]). The normal earns its place not by describing raw data but
by being the distribution of **averages and sums** of many independent contributions (the
Central Limit Theorem) and by being **closed under the linear operations** statistics runs
on. It is the distribution of *processed* data — estimates, totals, means — far more
reliably than of *raw* data. Volunteer that distinction and you have answered the deeper
question.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Strip the constants away and look at the heart of the curve:
$$
f(x)\ \propto\ \exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\Bigl(\tfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\Bigr)^2\Bigr).
$$
Read it as a story in three moves, none of which needs a symbol manipulated yet.

1. **$(x-\mu)$** measures how far $x$ is from the center $\mu$. The curve only cares about
   *distance from the center*, so it is automatically **symmetric**: $x=\mu+3$ and
   $x=\mu-3$ get identical heights. Errors above and below the truth are equally likely.
2. **The square, $(x-\mu)^2$**, says a deviation of size $2$ is penalized *four* times as
   hard as a deviation of size $1$ — the same quadratic penalty that defines least squares.
   Big errors are not just less likely, they are *disproportionately* less likely.
3. **The $\exp(-\,\cdot\,)$** turns that growing penalty into a *shrinking* height, and it
   shrinks **fast** — faster than any polynomial. This is the source of both the curve's
   greatest strength (everything is finite and tractable) and its greatest danger (the
   tails are absurdly light, @sec-failure).

The role of $\sigma$ is set by the ratio $(x-\mu)/\sigma$: it is a *yardstick*. Measuring
the deviation in units of $\sigma$ means the curve has the *same shape* for every $\sigma$,
just stretched horizontally. Double $\sigma$ and the hump gets twice as wide; the height
out front, $1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}$, drops to keep the total area at $1$. @fig-shapes shows
this: change $\mu$ to slide the curve, change $\sigma$ to stretch it, and nothing else
about its form ever changes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-shapes
#| fig-cap: "The two parameters do exactly two things. **Left:** changing the mean $\\mu$ slides the identical bell along the axis without reshaping it. **Right:** changing the standard deviation $\\sigma$ stretches it — larger $\\sigma$ is wider and shorter (the height drops as $1/\\sigma$ to keep the enclosed area equal to $1$). Every normal curve is the same curve under a shift and a stretch."
x = np.linspace(-8, 12, 600)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))

for mu, c in [(0.0, CURVE), (3.0, CURVE2), (6.0, ACCENT)]:
    axL.plot(x, normal_pdf(x, mu, 1.5), color=c, label=fr"$\mu={mu:.0f},\ \sigma=1.5$")
axL.set_title(r"Changing $\mu$ slides the curve")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("density $f(x)$")
axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

for sg, c in [(0.7, CURVE), (1.5, CURVE2), (3.0, ACCENT)]:
    axR.plot(x, normal_pdf(x, 2.0, sg), color=c, label=fr"$\mu=2,\ \sigma={sg}$")
axR.set_title(r"Changing $\sigma$ stretches the curve")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("density $f(x)$")
axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The whole formal job of the article is now visible. We have a *shape*,
$\exp(-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2)$. To make it a legitimate probability density it must enclose
total area $1$ ([PROB-4]). So we must find the one number $c$ for which
$c\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\exp(-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2)\,dx = 1$. Computing that integral —
which has no elementary antiderivative and so cannot be done by the Calc-1 method of
finding an antiderivative — is the central technical event of the article, and it is where
the famous $\sqrt{2\pi}$ comes from.

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

## The definition, with the constant left as a mystery {#sec-def}

::: {#def-normal}
**Definition (normal distribution).** A continuous random variable $X$ is **normally
distributed** with mean $\mu\in\mathbb R$ and variance $\sigma^2>0$, written
$X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$, if its probability density function is
$$
f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr),
\qquad x\in\mathbb R.
$$ {#eq-density}
The special case $\mu=0,\ \sigma^2=1$ is the **standard normal**, with density
denoted $\varphi$ and CDF denoted $\Phi$:
$$
\varphi(z)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-z^2/2},
\qquad
\Phi(z)=\int_{-\infty}^{z}\varphi(t)\,dt.
$$ {#eq-std-density}
:::

Every symbol earns its keep. $\mu$ is the **location** (we will *prove* it is the mean in
@sec-mean); $\sigma^2$ is the **scale-squared** (we will *prove* it is the variance in
@sec-variance); the exponential supplies the bell shape from @sec-intuition; and the front
factor $1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}$ is the **normalizing constant** whose only job is to force
the total area to $1$. Right now that constant is asserted, not earned. We do not assert
things in this series, so we now derive it.

A note on $\Phi$: the standard-normal CDF in @eq-std-density genuinely has *no* closed
form — there is no finite combination of elementary functions equal to it. This is not a
gap in our knowledge; it is a theorem (the antiderivative of $e^{-t^2}$ is non-elementary).
We therefore compute $\Phi$ numerically and tabulate it; that is why "$z$-tables" exist.
We will use $\Phi$ freely below as a *defined* object, the area to the left under the
standard bell.

## Deriving the normalizing constant: the Gaussian integral {#sec-gaussian-integral}

The entire definition stands or falls on one claim: that the area under the bare bell shape
is finite and equal to a specific number. We prove it for the standard case; the general
case follows by a substitution we also do in full. This is the one place in the article we
reach above the Calculus-I floor — to a *double* integral evaluated in *polar
coordinates* — so we build that tool as we use it and flag it explicitly.

::: {#thm-gaussian-integral}
**Theorem (the Gaussian integral).**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2/2}\,dx \;=\; \sqrt{2\pi}.
$$ {#eq-gaussian-integral}
:::

::: {.proof}
Call the unknown value $I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2/2}\,dx$. It is a positive number
(the integrand is positive and the area is finite, since $e^{-x^2/2}$ decays faster than,
say, $1/x^2$ in the tails). We cannot attack $I$ directly — $e^{-x^2/2}$ has no elementary
antiderivative, so the Calc-1 fundamental-theorem route is closed. The trick, due to
Poisson, is to compute **$I^2$** instead, because squaring turns one stubborn
one-dimensional integral into a two-dimensional one that polar coordinates crack open.

**Step 1 — write $I^2$ as a double integral.** Since $I$ is just a number, $I^2=I\cdot I$.
Write the two copies with *different* dummy variables, $x$ for one and $y$ for the other —
legitimate, because the name of the variable of integration is irrelevant:
$$
I^2=\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2/2}\,dx\right)
     \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-y^2/2}\,dy\right).
$$
The first integral is a constant with respect to $y$, so we may slide it *inside* the
$y$-integral, and then the $x$-integral's integrand is constant with respect to $y$ so it
slides inside too:
$$
I^2=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2/2}\,dx\right)e^{-y^2/2}\,dy
   =\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2/2}\,e^{-y^2/2}\,dx\,dy.
$$
Now combine the two exponentials with the rule $e^a e^b=e^{a+b}$:
$$
I^2=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
      e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}\,dx\,dy.
$$ {#eq-double}
This is a double integral over the entire $xy$-plane. (A **double integral** is the
straightforward two-variable cousin of the Calc-1 integral: it sums the value
$e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}$ over every little patch $dx\,dy$ of the plane. Its value is the *volume*
under the surface $z=e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}$, just as a single integral is the area under a
curve. That is all we need to know about it.) The flag: this surface and its volume are the
one above-floor object in the article.

**Step 2 — recognize the symmetry.** Look at the integrand $e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}$. It depends
on $x$ and $y$ *only through* $x^2+y^2$, which is the squared distance from the origin.
So the surface is perfectly circular — a radially symmetric bell mound over the plane, the
same height at every point a given distance from the origin. A function with circular
symmetry is begging to be integrated in **polar coordinates**, where distance-from-origin
is itself one of the coordinates.

**Step 3 — change to polar coordinates.** Replace the Cartesian pair $(x,y)$ with the polar
pair $(r,\theta)$, where $r\ge 0$ is the distance from the origin and
$\theta\in[0,2\pi)$ is the angle, related by
$$
x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta,\qquad x^2+y^2=r^2.
$$
The last equality is the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1$:
$x^2+y^2=r^2\cos^2\theta+r^2\sin^2\theta=r^2(\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta)=r^2$. So the
integrand becomes simply $e^{-r^2/2}$.

We need one more ingredient: when we trade $dx\,dy$ for $dr\,d\theta$, the area element is
**not** $dr\,d\theta$ but $r\,dr\,d\theta$. Here is why, at the floor's level, no Jacobian
machinery assumed. A little patch of the plane between radii $r$ and $r+dr$ and between
angles $\theta$ and $\theta+d\theta$ is, to first order, a tiny rectangle: its *radial*
side has length $dr$, and its *tangential* side is a small arc of a circle of radius $r$
subtending angle $d\theta$, whose length is (radius)$\times$(angle) $=r\,d\theta$. The area
of that little rectangle is therefore $dr\times r\,d\theta=r\,dr\,d\theta$. The extra
factor of $r$ is just "the farther out you are, the more area each degree of angle sweeps."
@fig-polar draws this patch.

Covering the whole plane means letting $r$ run from $0$ to $\infty$ and $\theta$ run from
$0$ to $2\pi$ (one full turn). So @eq-double becomes
$$
I^2=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-r^2/2}\,\underbrace{r\,dr}_{\text{radial}}\,d\theta.
$$

**Step 4 — now it is elementary.** The reason the whole trick works: that stray factor of
$r$ is exactly the derivative-friendly piece that makes the inner integral a routine
Calc-1 substitution. Hold $\theta$ fixed and do the inner integral over $r$. Substitute
$u=r^2/2$, so $du=r\,dr$ — and there is the $r\,dr$, waiting for us. When $r=0$, $u=0$; as
$r\to\infty$, $u\to\infty$. Thus
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-r^2/2}\,r\,dr
=\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u}\,du
=\Bigl[-e^{-u}\Bigr]_{0}^{\infty}
=\bigl(0\bigr)-\bigl(-1\bigr)=1.
$$
The inner integral is just $1$. The outer integral is then immediate — integrating the
constant $1$ over $\theta\in[0,2\pi)$ multiplies it by the length $2\pi$ of that interval:
$$
I^2=\int_{0}^{2\pi} 1\,d\theta = 2\pi.
$$

**Step 5 — finish.** We have $I^2=2\pi$, and $I>0$ (it is the integral of a positive
function), so taking the positive square root,
$$
I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2/2}\,dx=\sqrt{2\pi}.\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-polar
#| fig-cap: "Where the $\\sqrt{2\\pi}$ comes from. We compute $I^2$ as the volume under the radially symmetric mound $e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}$ over the plane. **Left:** the mound; its circular symmetry is the whole point. **Right:** the polar area patch — radial side $dr$, tangential arc $r\\,d\\theta$, area $r\\,dr\\,d\\theta$. That extra factor of $r$ is exactly what makes the radial integral a one-line substitution, yielding $I^2=2\\pi$."
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8.8, 3.9))

axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection="3d")
gx = np.linspace(-3, 3, 60)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gx)
Z = np.exp(-(GX**2 + GY**2) / 2.0)
axL.plot_surface(GX, GY, Z, cmap="viridis", linewidth=0, antialiased=True, alpha=0.9)
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$")
axL.set_zlabel(r"$e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2}$")
axL.set_title("Radially symmetric mound")
axL.view_init(elev=32, azim=-122)

axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
r0, dr, th0, dth = 1.6, 0.55, 0.55, 0.6
ang = np.linspace(th0, th0 + dth, 40)
# the four bounding arcs/segments of the polar patch
axR.plot(r0*np.cos(ang), r0*np.sin(ang), color=CURVE, lw=2)
axR.plot((r0+dr)*np.cos(ang), (r0+dr)*np.sin(ang), color=CURVE, lw=2)
axR.plot([r0*np.cos(th0), (r0+dr)*np.cos(th0)],
         [r0*np.sin(th0), (r0+dr)*np.sin(th0)], color=CURVE, lw=2)
axR.plot([r0*np.cos(th0+dth), (r0+dr)*np.cos(th0+dth)],
         [r0*np.sin(th0+dth), (r0+dr)*np.sin(th0+dth)], color=CURVE, lw=2)
# light radial grid
for tt in np.linspace(0, np.pi/2, 5):
    axR.plot([0, 2.6*np.cos(tt)], [0, 2.6*np.sin(tt)], color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4)
axR.annotate(r"$dr$", (1.95*np.cos(th0+dth/2), 1.95*np.sin(th0+dth/2)),
             color=CURVE2, fontsize=11)
axR.annotate(r"$r\,d\theta$", (r0*np.cos(th0+dth)*0.78, r0*np.sin(th0+dth)+0.16),
             color=ACCENT, fontsize=11)
axR.set_xlim(0, 2.6); axR.set_ylim(0, 2.6); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y$")
axR.set_title("Polar area patch  $r\\,dr\\,d\\theta$")
axR.grid(False)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Now we cash the theorem in. We must confirm that @eq-density really integrates to $1$.
Take the general density and substitute the **standardizing** change of variable
$z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$, so $x=\mu+\sigma z$ and $dx=\sigma\,dz$ (a Calc-1 linear substitution;
the limits stay $-\infty$ to $\infty$ since $z\to\pm\infty$ as $x\to\pm\infty$):
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
   \exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr)dx
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2}\,\sigma\,dz
=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz.
$$
The exponent simplified because $(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)=(\sigma z)^2/(2\sigma^2)=z^2/2$. The
front factor simplifies with $\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}=\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma$ (since
$\sigma>0$), so $\sigma/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}=\sigma/(\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma)=1/\sqrt{2\pi}$.
Using @eq-gaussian-integral for the remaining integral,
$$
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\cdot\sqrt{2\pi}=1.
$$
The area is exactly $1$. The constant $1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}$ was *forced*: it is precisely
the number that cancels the $\sqrt{2\pi}$ the geometry of the plane produced. There was
never any freedom in it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the $\pi$ in a probability distribution possibly come from? There are no
circles here."* There is a circle — you just have to square the integral to see it.
Computing $I^2$ turns the problem into a volume over the plane, and the integrand's
**circular symmetry** ($x^2+y^2=r^2$) is what invites polar coordinates; the $2\pi$ is
literally the radians in one full turn around that circle, surviving from the
$\int_0^{2\pi}d\theta$. The appearance of $\pi$ is the signature of that hidden rotational
symmetry. This is a favorite interview question precisely because the answer is so
satisfying: the $\pi$ is geometric, not probabilistic.
:::

## $\mu$ really is the mean {#sec-mean}

We named the parameter $\mu$ "the mean" in @def-normal, but a name is not a proof. The
expectation of a continuous random variable is $E[X]=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x f(x)\,dx$
([PROB-5]). We compute it.

::: {#thm-mean}
**Theorem (mean of the normal).** If $X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ then $E[X]=\mu$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By definition of expectation for a continuous variable ([PROB-5]),
$$
E[X]=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
   \exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr)dx.
$$
Substitute the standardizing change $z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$ again, so $x=\mu+\sigma z$ and
$dx=\sigma\,dz$. As in @sec-gaussian-integral the front factor and the $\sigma$ from $dx$
collapse the constant to $1/\sqrt{2\pi}$, and the exponent becomes $-z^2/2$:
$$
E[X]=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}(\mu+\sigma z)\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz.
$$
Split the integral across the sum $\mu+\sigma z$ — legal because integration is linear:
$$
E[X]=\mu\cdot\underbrace{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-z^2/2}\,dz}_{(A)}
     \;+\;\sigma\cdot\underbrace{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}z\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz}_{(B)}.
$$
Term $(A)$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\sqrt{2\pi}=1$ by the Gaussian integral
@eq-gaussian-integral.

Term $(B)$ is $0$, and we show it two ways so nothing is taken on faith. *By symmetry:* the
function $g(z)=z\,e^{-z^2/2}$ is **odd**, meaning $g(-z)=-g(z)$ (because $-z$ flips the sign
of the $z$ in front while leaving $e^{-z^2/2}$ unchanged), and the integral of any odd
function over a symmetric interval $(-\infty,\infty)$ cancels to zero — every contribution
at $+z$ is undone by its mirror at $-z$. *By antiderivative,* to be fully explicit: the
chain rule gives $\frac{d}{dz}\bigl(-e^{-z^2/2}\bigr)=z\,e^{-z^2/2}$, so
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}z\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz
=\Bigl[-e^{-z^2/2}\Bigr]_{-\infty}^{\infty}
=\bigl(0\bigr)-\bigl(0\bigr)=0,
$$
since $e^{-z^2/2}\to 0$ at both ends. Either way $(B)=0$. Therefore
$$
E[X]=\mu\cdot 1+\sigma\cdot 0=\mu.\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

The mean is $\mu$ because the curve is symmetric about $\mu$ — the balance point of a
symmetric mass sits at its center of symmetry. Term $(B)$ vanishing *is* that symmetry,
made arithmetic.

## $\sigma^2$ really is the variance {#sec-variance}

Likewise we must earn the name "variance." The variance is
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[(X-\mu)^2]$ ([PROB-6]). The computation needs one genuine tool
beyond the last — **integration by parts** — which is a Calc-1 technique, so we state it
and use it openly.

::: {#thm-variance}
**Theorem (variance of the normal).** If $X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ then
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma^2$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By definition ([PROB-6]) and the same substitution $z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$ (now $(x-\mu)^2$
becomes $\sigma^2 z^2$, and the constant collapses to $1/\sqrt{2\pi}$ as before):
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)=E\bigl[(X-\mu)^2\bigr]
=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}(x-\mu)^2\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
   \exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\Bigr)dx
=\frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz.
$$
Everything now rides on the integral $J=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}z^2 e^{-z^2/2}\,dz$. We
evaluate it by **integration by parts**, $\int u\,dv = uv-\int v\,du$. The clever split is
to write $z^2 e^{-z^2/2}$ as $z\cdot\bigl(z\,e^{-z^2/2}\bigr)$ and set
$$
u=z,\qquad dv = z\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz.
$$
Then $du=dz$, and $v=\int z\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz=-e^{-z^2/2}$ (the antiderivative we already
found in @sec-mean). Apply the parts formula:
$$
J=\Bigl[\,\underbrace{z\cdot\bigl(-e^{-z^2/2}\bigr)}_{uv}\,\Bigr]_{-\infty}^{\infty}
  -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\bigl(-e^{-z^2/2}\bigr)\,dz.
$$
The boundary term $\bigl[-z\,e^{-z^2/2}\bigr]_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ is $0$: as
$z\to\pm\infty$, the exponential $e^{-z^2/2}$ crushes the linear $z$ to zero (the
exponential decays faster than any power of $z$ grows). The remaining integral is
$$
J=0+\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz=\sqrt{2\pi},
$$
again by the Gaussian integral @eq-gaussian-integral. Substitute back:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)=\frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\cdot J
=\frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\cdot\sqrt{2\pi}=\sigma^2.\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

So the parameters are exactly what their names promised — and notice that the *same*
Gaussian integral that normalized the density also delivered both moments. The constant
$\sqrt{2\pi}$ is the keystone of the entire distribution.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the most probable single value of a normal variable, and is it the mean?"* The
density @eq-density is largest where the exponent is largest, i.e. where $(x-\mu)^2$ is
*smallest*, which is at $x=\mu$. So the **mode** is $\mu$. Because the curve is symmetric,
the **median** (the point with half the area on each side) is also $\mu$. For the normal,
mean = median = mode = $\mu$ — and this is no accident but a *forced consequence* of two
features we have already nailed down: unimodality (the single peak) pins the mode at the
center, and symmetry about $\mu$ (the mean computation of @sec-mean) pins the mean and the
median there too. They coincide *because* the curve is symmetric and single-peaked, and they
come emphatically *apart* for the skewed distributions in [PROB-14], where neither condition
holds and separating the three is the whole point.
:::

## Standardization: every normal is the standard normal in disguise {#sec-standardize}

We have used the substitution $z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$ three times to evaluate integrals. It is
worth elevating to a named result, because it is how *all* normal probabilities are
actually computed — by reducing any normal to the single tabulated standard one.

::: {#thm-standardize}
**Theorem (standardization).** If $X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ then the standardized
variable
$$
Z=\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}
$$
is standard normal, $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$. Consequently, for any $a<b$,
$$
P(a\le X\le b)=\Phi\!\Bigl(\frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\Bigr)-\Phi\!\Bigl(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\Bigr).
$$ {#eq-standardize}
:::

::: {.proof}
We find the density of $Z$ by going through its CDF — the cleanest fully-explicit route
for a monotone transform ([PROB-4]). For any number $z$,
$$
F_Z(z)=P(Z\le z)=P\!\Bigl(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\le z\Bigr)
=P\bigl(X\le \mu+\sigma z\bigr)=F_X(\mu+\sigma z),
$$
where the third step multiplied both sides of the inequality by $\sigma>0$ (which
preserves the direction of $\le$) and added $\mu$. Differentiate both ends with respect to
$z$ to get the density of $Z$, using the chain rule on the right (the inner derivative of
$\mu+\sigma z$ is $\sigma$):
$$
f_Z(z)=f_X(\mu+\sigma z)\cdot\sigma.
$$
Now plug in $f_X$ from @eq-density at the point $x=\mu+\sigma z$. There the deviation is
$x-\mu=\sigma z$, so $(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)=z^2/2$, and
$$
f_Z(z)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,e^{-z^2/2}\cdot\sigma
=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\,e^{-z^2/2}
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-z^2/2}=\varphi(z).
$$
That is exactly the standard-normal density @eq-std-density, so $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$. The
probability statement @eq-standardize then follows: $a\le X\le b$ is the same event as
$\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\le Z\le\frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}$, whose probability is the difference of
the standard CDF $\Phi$ at the two endpoints. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is why a single $z$-table (or one `Phi` function in code) suffices for *every* normal
problem: subtract the mean, divide by the standard deviation, look up $\Phi$. It is also the
origin of the famous **68–95–99.7 rule** — within $1$, $2$, $3$ standard deviations of the
mean lie about $68\%$, $95\%$, $99.7\%$ of the mass — which is nothing more than
$\Phi(1)-\Phi(-1)$, $\Phi(2)-\Phi(-2)$, $\Phi(3)-\Phi(-3)$. @fig-empirical computes those
exact areas.

```{python}
#| label: fig-empirical
#| fig-cap: "The 68–95–99.7 rule is just three values of $\\Phi$. Shaded bands show the probability within $1$, $2$, and $3$ standard deviations of the mean for the standard normal; the printed numbers are the exact areas $\\Phi(k)-\\Phi(-k)$. Because of standardization (@thm-standardize) these percentages hold for *every* normal, whatever its $\\mu$ and $\\sigma$."
from scipy.stats import norm

z = np.linspace(-4, 4, 800)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.0))
ax.plot(z, norm.pdf(z), color=CURVE)
bands = [(1, "#bcd0e8", 0.9), (2, "#dbe6f2", 0.55), (3, "#eef3f9", 0.35)]
for k, col, a in reversed(bands):
    mask = (z >= -k) & (z <= k)
    ax.fill_between(z[mask], norm.pdf(z[mask]), color=col, alpha=a, zorder=0)
for k in (1, 2, 3):
    p = norm.cdf(k) - norm.cdf(-k)
    ax.annotate(fr"$\pm{k}\sigma:\ {100*p:.1f}\%$",
                xy=(0, norm.pdf(k) + 0.012 + 0.045*(k-1)),
                ha="center", fontsize=10, color=CURVE)
ax.axvline(0, color=ACCENT, lw=1.2, ls="--")
ax.set_xlabel(r"standardized value $z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$")
ax.set_ylabel("density $\\varphi(z)$")
ax.set_title("The 68–95–99.7 rule")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"P(|Z| <= 1) = {norm.cdf(1)-norm.cdf(-1):.4f}")
print(f"P(|Z| <= 2) = {norm.cdf(2)-norm.cdf(-2):.4f}")
print(f"P(|Z| <= 3) = {norm.cdf(3)-norm.cdf(-3):.4f}")
```

## The defining property: independent normals add to a normal {#sec-closure}

Now the structural fact that, more than the bell shape, is *why* the normal sits at the
center of statistics. Add two independent normals and you get a normal — not approximately,
exactly — with the means and variances simply adding. No other family of distributions is
so perfectly stable under addition while keeping a free location and scale.

::: {#thm-closure}
**Theorem (closure under independent sums).** Let $X_1\sim\mathcal N(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2)$ and
$X_2\sim\mathcal N(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)$ be **independent**. Then
$$
X_1+X_2\ \sim\ \mathcal N\bigl(\mu_1+\mu_2,\ \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2\bigr).
$$
:::

We first prove the **means and variances add** — which holds even more generally — and
then prove the harder part, that the *shape stays normal*.

That the parameters add needs no normality at all. For *any* random variables,
expectation is linear ([PROB-5]), so $E[X_1+X_2]=E[X_1]+E[X_2]=\mu_1+\mu_2$. And for
*independent* variables the variance of a sum is the sum of the variances ([PROB-8]),
$\operatorname{Var}(X_1+X_2)=\operatorname{Var}(X_1)+\operatorname{Var}(X_2)=\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2$.
So *whatever* $X_1+X_2$ turns out to be, its mean and variance are pinned. The real content
of the theorem is that the sum is again **normal** — that the shape survives.

::: {.proof}
**Setup.** The density of a sum of independent continuous variables is the **convolution**
of their densities ([PROB-8]): if $S=X_1+X_2$, then for the value $S=s$, we sum over all
the ways $X_1=t$ and $X_2=s-t$ can combine,
$$
f_S(s)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X_1}(t)\,f_{X_2}(s-t)\,dt.
$$
To keep the algebra readable we prove the case $\mu_1=\mu_2=0$; the general case is this
result shifted, since adding constants $\mu_1,\mu_2$ just slides each variable (and we
already know the means add). Write $\sigma_1^2=a$ and $\sigma_2^2=b$ for brevity. Then
$$
f_S(s)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
   \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi a}}e^{-t^2/2a}\,
   \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi b}}e^{-(s-t)^2/2b}\,dt
=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ab}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
   \exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12\bigl[\tfrac{t^2}{a}+\tfrac{(s-t)^2}{b}\bigr]\Bigr)dt.
$$

**The whole game is the exponent.** Everything hinges on rewriting the bracket
$Q(t)=\dfrac{t^2}{a}+\dfrac{(s-t)^2}{b}$ as a perfect square in $t$ plus a leftover that
does not involve $t$ — the algebraic move called **completing the square**. First put it
over the common denominator $ab$ and expand $(s-t)^2=s^2-2st+t^2$:
$$
Q(t)=\frac{b\,t^2+a\,(s-t)^2}{ab}
=\frac{b t^2 + a t^2 - 2a s t + a s^2}{ab}
=\frac{(a+b)\,t^2 - 2as\,t + a s^2}{ab}.
$$
Factor the coefficient $(a+b)/ab$ out of the two $t$-terms so the leading coefficient of
$t^2$ inside is $1$:
$$
Q(t)=\frac{a+b}{ab}\left[\,t^2-\frac{2as}{a+b}\,t\,\right]+\frac{a s^2}{ab}.
$$
Complete the square inside the bracket. For $t^2-2\beta t$ we add and subtract $\beta^2$ to
form $(t-\beta)^2-\beta^2$; here $\beta=\dfrac{as}{a+b}$:
$$
t^2-\frac{2as}{a+b}t=\Bigl(t-\frac{as}{a+b}\Bigr)^2-\Bigl(\frac{as}{a+b}\Bigr)^2.
$$
Substitute back:
$$
Q(t)=\frac{a+b}{ab}\Bigl(t-\frac{as}{a+b}\Bigr)^2
     -\frac{a+b}{ab}\cdot\frac{a^2 s^2}{(a+b)^2}
     +\frac{a s^2}{ab}.
$$
Now simplify the two leftover ($t$-free) terms. The middle term is
$-\dfrac{a^2 s^2}{ab(a+b)}=-\dfrac{a s^2}{b(a+b)}$. Combine with the last term
$\dfrac{a s^2}{ab}=\dfrac{s^2}{b}$ over the common denominator $b(a+b)$:
$$
-\frac{a s^2}{b(a+b)}+\frac{s^2}{b}
=\frac{-a s^2 + s^2(a+b)}{b(a+b)}
=\frac{s^2\,b}{b(a+b)}
=\frac{s^2}{a+b}.
$$
So, cleanly,
$$
Q(t)=\frac{a+b}{ab}\Bigl(t-\frac{as}{a+b}\Bigr)^2+\frac{s^2}{a+b}.
$$
The first piece carries *all* the $t$-dependence; the second, $s^2/(a+b)$, is constant in
$t$ and can leave the integral.

**Do the integral.** Insert this $Q(t)$ and pull the $t$-free factor out:
$$
f_S(s)=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ab}}\,e^{-s^2/2(a+b)}
   \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{a+b}{ab}
   \Bigl(t-\frac{as}{a+b}\Bigr)^2\Bigr)dt.
$$
The remaining integral is a Gaussian integral in $t$. It has the form
$\int e^{-(t-c)^2/2v}\,dt$ with center $c=as/(a+b)$ and "variance" $v=ab/(a+b)$, and we
already know (it is the normalization computation of @sec-gaussian-integral, run in
reverse) that
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-(t-c)^2/2v}\,dt=\sqrt{2\pi v}.
$$
(Explicitly: substitute $w=(t-c)/\sqrt v$, $dt=\sqrt v\,dw$, giving
$\sqrt v\int e^{-w^2/2}dw=\sqrt v\,\sqrt{2\pi}=\sqrt{2\pi v}$.) Here
$\sqrt{2\pi v}=\sqrt{2\pi\,ab/(a+b)}$. Therefore
$$
f_S(s)=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ab}}\,e^{-s^2/2(a+b)}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{2\pi ab}{a+b}}.
$$
Collect the constants out front: $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ab}}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi ab}{a+b}}
=\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{ab}}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{2\pi}\sqrt{ab}}{\sqrt{a+b}}
=\dfrac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2\pi\sqrt{a+b}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi(a+b)}}$. Hence
$$
f_S(s)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi(a+b)}}\,\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{s^2}{2(a+b)}\Bigr).
$$
This is *exactly* the normal density @eq-density with mean $0$ and variance $a+b$. So
$S=X_1+X_2\sim\mathcal N(0,\,\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$ in the centered case, and restoring the
means slides it to $\mathcal N(\mu_1+\mu_2,\ \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This proof was long, and that length is the point: the shape's survival is a real algebraic
miracle (the completed square *is* a normal density in $t$), not a slogan. There is a
shorter route through moment generating functions, which [PROB-20] takes — but it is shorter
only because it borrows machinery built there; here we did it with the bare convolution
integral and Calc-1 algebra, owing nothing.

Two consequences follow immediately, and they are the ones every downstream track uses.

::: {#cor-affine}
**Corollary (affine transforms and i.i.d. means).** Let $X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ and
let $a\neq 0,b$ be constants. Then $aX+b\sim\mathcal N(a\mu+b,\ a^2\sigma^2)$. Consequently,
if $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are i.i.d. $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$, the sample mean is
$$
\bar X=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\ \sim\ \mathcal N\!\Bigl(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\Bigr).
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
**The affine claim.** Set $Y=aX+b$ with $a\neq 0$. We find the density of $Y$ by the same
CDF route used in @thm-standardize, but now we must handle the *sign* of $a$ explicitly,
because dividing an inequality by a negative number flips its direction — a step the
standardization proof never faced (there the divisor $1/\sigma$ was positive). We take the
two cases in turn and watch them reunite.

*Case $a>0$.* For any number $y$, dividing the inequality $aX+b\le y$ by the positive $a$
keeps its direction:
$$
F_Y(y)=P(aX+b\le y)=P\!\Bigl(X\le \frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr)=F_X\!\Bigl(\frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr).
$$
Differentiate both ends in $y$, using the chain rule on the right (the inner derivative of
$(y-b)/a$ is $1/a$):
$$
f_Y(y)=f_X\!\Bigl(\frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr)\cdot\frac1a .
$$

*Case $a<0$.* Now dividing $aX+b\le y$ by the negative $a$ *reverses* the inequality:
$$
F_Y(y)=P(aX+b\le y)=P\!\Bigl(X\ge \frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr)=1-F_X\!\Bigl(\frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr),
$$
where the last step used $P(X\ge c)=1-P(X<c)=1-F_X(c)$ (the event $X<c$ and $X\ge c$
partition the line, and $P(X<c)=F_X(c)$ for a continuous variable, since the single point
$c$ carries no probability, [PROB-4]). Differentiate in $y$; the chain rule again brings
down the inner derivative $1/a$, and the leading $1$ differentiates to $0$:
$$
f_Y(y)=-\,f_X\!\Bigl(\frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr)\cdot\frac1a .
$$

*The two cases are one formula.* When $a>0$, $1/a=1/|a|$; when $a<0$, $-1/a=1/|a|$. So both
cases collapse to the single statement
$$
f_Y(y)=\frac{1}{|a|}\,f_X\!\Bigl(\frac{y-b}{a}\Bigr).
$$
Now substitute $f_X$ from @eq-density at the point $x=(y-b)/a$. The deviation there is
$$
x-\mu=\frac{y-b}{a}-\mu=\frac{y-b-a\mu}{a}=\frac{y-(a\mu+b)}{a},
$$
so, squaring and dividing by $2\sigma^2$,
$$
\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}=\frac{\bigl(y-(a\mu+b)\bigr)^2}{2\,a^2\sigma^2}.
$$
The front factor picks up the $1/|a|$: using $\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}=\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma$
(since $\sigma>0$) and $|a|\,\sigma=\sqrt{a^2\sigma^2}$,
$$
\frac{1}{|a|}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
=\frac{1}{|a|\,\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sqrt{a^2\sigma^2}}
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\,a^2\sigma^2}}.
$$
Assembling the two pieces,
$$
f_Y(y)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\,a^2\sigma^2}}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{\bigl(y-(a\mu+b)\bigr)^2}{2\,a^2\sigma^2}\Bigr),
$$
which is *exactly* the normal density @eq-density with mean $a\mu+b$ and variance
$a^2\sigma^2$. Hence $aX+b\sim\mathcal N(a\mu+b,\ a^2\sigma^2)$ — the shape is not merely
inferred from its mean and variance, it is read straight off the density. (As a consistency
check, those parameters match the model-free identities $E[aX+b]=a\mu+b$ by linearity
([PROB-5]) and $\operatorname{Var}(aX+b)=a^2\sigma^2$ by the scaling rule for variance
([PROB-6]); the derivation above is what additionally certifies the result is *normal*.)

**The i.i.d. mean.** Each $X_i\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ and they are independent;
summing $n$ of them, @thm-closure (applied $n-1$ times, folding in one more independent
normal at each step) gives $\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\sim\mathcal N(n\mu,\ n\sigma^2)$. Then
$\bar X=\frac1n\sum X_i$ is the affine transform $aS+b$ of $S=\sum X_i$ with $a=1/n$ and
$b=0$, so the affine claim just proved gives
$$
\bar X\sim\mathcal N\!\Bigl(\tfrac1n\cdot n\mu,\ \tfrac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2\Bigr)
=\mathcal N\!\Bigl(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\Bigr). \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

That last result is the quiet workhorse of all of inference: the sample mean of normal data
is *itself* normal, centered at the true $\mu$, with its spread shrinking like
$\sigma/\sqrt n$. The $\sqrt n$ — not $n$ — is exactly the rate flagged back in [PROB-8].
@fig-closure shows the closure property and this shrinking in action.

```{python}
#| label: fig-closure
#| fig-cap: "Closure under addition and averaging, verified by simulation. **Left:** the histogram of $X_1+X_2$ for independent $X_1\\sim\\mathcal N(1,1)$ and $X_2\\sim\\mathcal N(3,4)$ (so $\\sigma_2^2=4$) lands exactly on the predicted $\\mathcal N(4,5)$ curve — means and variances added. **Right:** the sampling distribution of $\\bar X$ for $n=1,4,16,64$ i.i.d. $\\mathcal N(0,1)$ draws; each is normal, centered at $0$, with standard deviation $1/\\sqrt n$ — the curve sharpens but never changes shape."
from scipy.stats import norm
rng = np.random.default_rng(13)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))

N = 200_000
s = rng.normal(1, 1, N) + rng.normal(3, 2, N)   # sigma2 = 2 => var 4
grid = np.linspace(s.min(), s.max(), 400)
axL.hist(s, bins=120, density=True, color="#cfd8e8", edgecolor="none")
axL.plot(grid, norm.pdf(grid, 4, np.sqrt(5)), color=CURVE2,
         label=r"predicted $\mathcal N(4,5)$")
axL.set_title(r"$X_1+X_2$ is normal, params add")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1+x_2$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

zz = np.linspace(-3, 3, 600)
for nn, c in [(1, GREY), (4, ACCENT), (16, CURVE), (64, CURVE2)]:
    axR.plot(zz, norm.pdf(zz, 0, 1/np.sqrt(nn)), color=c, label=fr"$n={nn}$")
axR.set_title(r"Sampling dist. of $\bar X$: SD $=1/\sqrt{n}$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\bar x$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Why it is *everywhere*: the Central Limit Theorem, previewed honestly {#sec-why-everywhere}

We can now say precisely why the bell curve keeps appearing — and be honest that the full
theorem is not ours to prove here. The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** says: if
$X_1,\dots,X_n$ are i.i.d. with *any* finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$ — not
necessarily normal — then the standardized sum converges to a standard normal as
$n\to\infty$:
$$
\frac{\bar X-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N(0,1).
$$
The closure property we just proved is the CLT's shadow: the normal is the *only* shape that
is perfectly stable under the averaging the CLT performs, so it is the only possible limit —
it is the **fixed point of averaging**. De Moivre's binomial-to-bell observation
(@sec-why) was the very first instance: the binomial is a sum of i.i.d. Bernoullis, so its
standardized version goes normal.

This is the honest origin of "the normal is everywhere." It is not that raw measurements are
bell-shaped; it is that anything built by **adding or averaging many independent small
contributions** ends up bell-shaped, regardless of the pieces' own distributions.
Measurement error is a sum of many tiny independent perturbations — hence Gaussian, hence
Gauss. @fig-clt watches a decidedly non-normal source (a coin-flip / Bernoulli) march toward
the bell as we sum more of it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-clt
#| fig-cap: "The Central Limit Theorem at work, starting from the *least* bell-shaped source there is. We average $n$ independent fair coin flips (each $0$ or $1$) and standardize. At $n=1$ the distribution is two spikes; by $n=2$ it is a triangle; by $n=10$ and $n=50$ it is visually indistinguishable from the standard normal (red). The normal emerges from summing, not from the pieces being normal — they could not be less normal."
from scipy.stats import norm
rng = np.random.default_rng(21)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8.6, 6.0))
zz = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 400)
for ax, n in zip(axes.ravel(), (1, 2, 10, 50)):
    flips = rng.integers(0, 2, size=(120_000, n)).mean(axis=1)
    # standardize: Bernoulli(0.5) has mean .5, var .25 => mean has var .25/n
    z = (flips - 0.5) / (0.5 / np.sqrt(n))
    ax.hist(z, bins=60, density=True, color="#cfd8e8", edgecolor="none")
    ax.plot(zz, norm.pdf(zz), color=CURVE2, lw=2)
    ax.set_title(fr"$n={n}$ flips averaged", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(-3.5, 3.5); ax.set_xlabel("standardized mean")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
The CLT is stated here, not proved. Its proper home is the **Statistical Inference** track
([INFR-A2]), which owns the limit theorems; the machinery it runs on — moment generating
and characteristic functions ([PROB-20]) and the modes of convergence behind the
"$\xrightarrow{d}$" arrow ([PROB-21]) — is built later in *this* track. We previewed the CLT
only to explain *why* the normal is central, because that "why" is the spine of the whole
article. The closure property (@thm-closure), by contrast, we proved in full and own
outright.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the distribution on real, honest problems — including one where it is the *wrong*
tool, so the boundary is visible and not a highlight reel.

## Example 1 — adult heights, computed by hand {#sec-heights}

Human adult heights within one sex are about as normal as real data gets, for exactly the
CLT reason: height is the sum of many small genetic and environmental contributions. For
U.S. adult men, height is well approximated by $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with $\mu=70$
inches and $\sigma=3$ inches. We answer three questions by hand, using only standardization
(@thm-standardize) and a $\Phi$-table (here, `scipy`'s `norm.cdf`).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-heights
#| echo: false
from scipy.stats import norm
mu, sigma = 70.0, 3.0

# Q1: P(X > 72)
z1 = (72 - mu) / sigma
p1 = 1 - norm.cdf(z1)
# Q2: P(68 <= X <= 74)
zl, zh = (68 - mu)/sigma, (74 - mu)/sigma
p2 = norm.cdf(zh) - norm.cdf(zl)
# Q3: the 95th percentile height
z95 = norm.ppf(0.95)
x95 = mu + sigma * z95

print(f"Q1  z = (72-70)/3        = {z1:.4f};  P(X>72) = 1-Phi(z) = {p1:.4f}")
print(f"Q2  z_low=(68-70)/3={zl:.4f},  z_high=(74-70)/3={zh:.4f}")
print(f"    P(68<=X<=74) = Phi({zh:.3f})-Phi({zl:.3f}) = {p2:.4f}")
print(f"Q3  z_0.95 = {z95:.4f};  x = 70 + 3*z = {x95:.3f} inches")
```

Walk the arithmetic the code printed, plugging straight into @eq-standardize:

- **What fraction of men are taller than 6 feet (72 in)?** Standardize the cutoff:
  $z=(72-70)/3=`{python} f"{z1:.3f}"`$. We want the right tail,
  $P(X>72)=1-\Phi(`{python} f"{z1:.3f}"`)=`{python} f"{p1:.4f}"`$ — about
  $`{python} f"{100*p1:.0f}"`\%$. The subtraction from $1$ is because $\Phi$ gives the area
  to the *left* and we want the area to the *right*.
- **What fraction are between 68 and 74 inches?** Standardize both ends:
  $z_{\text{low}}=(68-70)/3=`{python} f"{zl:.3f}"`$ and
  $z_{\text{high}}=(74-70)/3=`{python} f"{zh:.3f}"`$. By @eq-standardize the answer is
  $\Phi(`{python} f"{zh:.3f}"`)-\Phi(`{python} f"{zl:.3f}"`)=`{python} f"{p2:.4f}"`$ — about
  $`{python} f"{100*p2:.0f}"`\%$. (These limits are roughly $\pm 1.33\sigma$, consistent
  with the 68–95 rule.)
- **How tall must a man be to be in the tallest 5%?** Now we invert: we need the value
  $x_{0.95}$ with $\Phi$ of its $z$-score equal to $0.95$. The $z$ with
  $\Phi(z)=0.95$ is $z_{0.95}=`{python} f"{z95:.3f}"`$ (read off the table, or `norm.ppf`),
  so $x_{0.95}=\mu+\sigma z_{0.95}=70+3(`{python} f"{z95:.3f}"`)=`{python} f"{x95:.2f}"`$
  inches. Un-standardizing — multiply by $\sigma$, add $\mu$ — turns a $z$-score back into a
  height.

@fig-heights shades these three regions.

```{python}
#| label: fig-heights
#| fig-cap: "Adult-male heights as $\\mathcal N(70,3^2)$. The three shaded regions are the three questions of @sec-heights: the right tail past $72''$ (about $25\\%$), the central band $68$–$74''$ (about $68\\%$), and the $95$th-percentile cutoff at $\\approx 74.9''$ (dashed). Every answer is one or two evaluations of $\\Phi$ after standardizing."
from scipy.stats import norm
mu, sigma = 70.0, 3.0
x = np.linspace(58, 82, 700)
pdf = norm.pdf(x, mu, sigma)

fig, (a1, a2, a3) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.2, 3.3), sharey=True)
for ax in (a1, a2, a3):
    ax.plot(x, pdf, color=CURVE)
    ax.set_xlabel("height (in)")
a1.fill_between(x[x>=72], pdf[x>=72], color="#f3c0c4")
a1.set_title(r"$P(X>72)\approx 25\%$", fontsize=10); a1.set_ylabel("density")
m = (x>=68)&(x<=74)
a2.fill_between(x[m], pdf[m], color="#bcd0e8")
a2.set_title(r"$P(68\le X\le74)\approx 68\%$", fontsize=10)
x95 = mu + sigma*norm.ppf(0.95)
a3.fill_between(x[x>=x95], pdf[x>=x95], color="#bfe3cf")
a3.axvline(x95, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.4)
a3.set_title(fr"95th pct $\approx {x95:.1f}''$", fontsize=10)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — summing independent normals: a manufacturing tolerance stack {#sec-tolerance}

This example shows @thm-closure paying off where engineers actually use it. A machined shaft
assembly stacks three independent components end to end. Each component's length is normal,
manufactured to its own mean and tolerance:
$$
L_1\sim\mathcal N(40,\ 0.05^2),\quad
L_2\sim\mathcal N(25,\ 0.04^2),\quad
L_3\sim\mathcal N(15,\ 0.03^2)\ \ (\text{mm}),
$$
and the lengths are independent (different machines, different runs). The total length is
$T=L_1+L_2+L_3$. The assembly is in spec if $T$ lands within $\pm 0.1$ mm of the nominal
$80$ mm. What fraction of assemblies pass?

By @thm-closure (extended to three terms by applying it twice), $T$ is normal with the means
and variances added — *variances*, not standard deviations, which is the trap:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-tolerance
#| echo: false
from scipy.stats import norm
means = np.array([40.0, 25.0, 15.0])
sds   = np.array([0.05, 0.04, 0.03])
mu_T  = means.sum()
var_T = (sds**2).sum()
sd_T  = np.sqrt(var_T)
zlo, zhi = (79.9 - mu_T)/sd_T, (80.1 - mu_T)/sd_T
p_pass = norm.cdf(zhi) - norm.cdf(zlo)
sd_wrong = sds.sum()  # the tempting but WRONG "add the SDs" answer
print(f"mu_T  = 40+25+15                      = {mu_T:.3f} mm")
print(f"var_T = 0.05^2+0.04^2+0.03^2          = {var_T:.5f} mm^2")
print(f"sd_T  = sqrt(var_T)                   = {sd_T:.5f} mm")
print(f"  (WRONG if you add SDs: {sd_wrong:.3f} mm -- do not do this)")
print(f"P(in spec) = Phi({zhi:.3f}) - Phi({zlo:.3f}) = {p_pass:.4f}")
```

The total mean is $\mu_T=40+25+15=`{python} f"{mu_T:.1f}"`$ mm. The total *variance* is
$\sigma_T^2=0.05^2+0.04^2+0.03^2=`{python} f"{var_T:.5f}"`$ mm$^2$, so the total standard
deviation is $\sigma_T=\sqrt{`{python} f"{var_T:.5f}"`}=`{python} f"{sd_T:.4f}"`$ mm. The
in-spec probability standardizes the $\pm 0.1$ window:
$$
P(79.9\le T\le 80.1)
=\Phi\!\Bigl(\tfrac{0.1}{`{python} f"{sd_T:.4f}"`}\Bigr)-\Phi\!\Bigl(\tfrac{-0.1}{`{python} f"{sd_T:.4f}"`}\Bigr)
=`{python} f"{p_pass:.4f}"`,
$$
about $`{python} f"{100*p_pass:.1f}"`\%$ pass. The figure shows the stacked distribution.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Variances add; standard deviations do not.** It is tempting to total the tolerances as
$0.05+0.04+0.03=`{python} f"{0.05+0.04+0.03:.2f}"`$ mm and call that the assembly's spread.
That is wrong, and it *overstates* the spread badly. The correct combination adds the
*squares*: $\sigma_T=\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2}=`{python} f"{sd_T:.4f}"`$ mm.
Independent errors partially cancel — some components run long while others run short — so
the assembly is *tighter* than the naive sum of tolerances suggests. This "root-sum-square"
rule is the entire basis of statistical tolerance analysis, and it is just @thm-closure read
out loud.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-tolerance
#| fig-cap: "Tolerance stack-up. The three independent component lengths (left, each normal with its own small spread) sum to a normal total $T\\sim\\mathcal N(80, 0.0707^2)$ (right). The shaded band is the $\\pm0.1$ mm spec window; its area is the pass rate. The total spread $0.0707$ mm is the root-sum-square of the parts, *smaller* than the naive sum $0.12$ mm of the tolerances."
from scipy.stats import norm
means = np.array([40.0, 25.0, 15.0]); sds = np.array([0.05, 0.04, 0.03])
mu_T, sd_T = means.sum(), np.sqrt((sds**2).sum())

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
for m, s, c, lab in zip(means, sds, [CURVE, ACCENT, CURVE2], ["$L_1$","$L_2$","$L_3$"]):
    xx = np.linspace(m-4*s, m+4*s, 300)
    axL.plot(xx - m, norm.pdf(xx, m, s), color=c, label=lab)  # centered to overlay
axL.set_title("Component spreads (centered)")
axL.set_xlabel("deviation from nominal (mm)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

xt = np.linspace(mu_T-4*sd_T, mu_T+4*sd_T, 500)
axR.plot(xt, norm.pdf(xt, mu_T, sd_T), color=CURVE)
m = (xt>=79.9)&(xt<=80.1)
axR.fill_between(xt[m], norm.pdf(xt[m], mu_T, sd_T), color="#bfe3cf")
axR.axvline(79.9, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.2); axR.axvline(80.1, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axR.set_title(r"Total $T\sim\mathcal N(80,\,0.0707^2)$")
axR.set_xlabel("total length (mm)"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: daily stock returns {#sec-returns}

Now the honest counterexample, the one that keeps the article from being a highlight reel.
Finance leaned on the normal model for decades — the Black–Scholes option-pricing formula
assumes log-returns are normal — and the assumption is *seductively* close to true in the
middle and catastrophically wrong in the tails. We fit a normal to real-looking daily
returns of a stock index and look at where it lies to us.

```{python}
#| label: fig-returns
#| fig-cap: "Why the normal fails on financial returns. We simulate daily log-returns from a heavy-tailed Student's-$t$ (the realistic model, [PROB-15]) and overlay the best-fit normal (matching mean and variance). **Left:** in the bulk the normal looks fine — this is the seduction. **Right:** the same data on a log-density scale exposes the tails: the empirical returns (bars) have far more extreme days than the normal (red) allows. The Q-Q-style mismatch in the tails is where real money is lost."
from scipy.stats import norm, t as student_t
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)

# Realistic heavy-tailed daily returns: Student-t with 3 df, scaled to ~1% daily vol
nu = 3
raw = student_t.rvs(nu, size=300_000, random_state=rng)
ret = 0.01 * raw / np.sqrt(nu/(nu-2))   # scale so SD ~ 1%
mu_f, sd_f = ret.mean(), ret.std()
grid = np.linspace(-0.08, 0.08, 600)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.hist(ret, bins=200, range=(-0.05, 0.05), density=True,
         color="#cfd8e8", edgecolor="none")
axL.plot(grid, norm.pdf(grid, mu_f, sd_f), color=CURVE2, label="best-fit normal")
axL.set_xlim(-0.05, 0.05)
axL.set_title("In the bulk, the normal looks fine")
axL.set_xlabel("daily return"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.hist(ret, bins=200, range=(-0.08, 0.08), density=True,
         color="#cfd8e8", edgecolor="none")
axR.plot(grid, norm.pdf(grid, mu_f, sd_f), color=CURVE2, label="best-fit normal")
axR.set_yscale("log"); axR.set_ylim(1e-2, 1e2)
axR.set_title("On a log scale, the tails betray it")
axR.set_xlabel("daily return"); axR.set_ylabel("density (log)")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

# How badly does the normal under-rate a big move?
big = 0.05  # a 5% down day
p_norm = norm.cdf(-big, mu_f, sd_f)
p_emp  = np.mean(ret <= -big)
print(f"P(daily return <= -5%):  normal says {p_norm:.2e},  data shows {p_emp:.2e}")
print(f"The normal under-rates this crash by a factor of ~{p_emp/p_norm:.0f}.")

# values reused in the prose below
p_norm_crash = norm.cdf(-0.05, 0.0, 0.01)
p_5sigma = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(5))
```

The normal model says a $5\%$ single-day drop has probability about
`{python} f"{p_norm_crash:.0e}"`
— effectively never. The heavy-tailed reality produces such days orders of magnitude more
often, as the printed factor shows. The exponential tail of @eq-density, which made every
integral in this article converge so cleanly, is precisely what makes the model *deny* the
existence of crashes that do, in fact, happen. **When you reach for something else:** for
heavy-tailed data use the Student's $t$ ([PROB-15]) or other fat-tailed families, model the
tail directly with extreme-value theory (flagged in [PROB-23]), or work with returns whose
tails you have actually checked rather than assumed. The lesson is the Core's liability made
concrete.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The normal is the right tool astonishingly often and the wrong tool in a few predictable
places. Knowing which is which is the difference between using it and being used by it.

## Thin tails: the central liability {#sec-thin-tails}

The exponential $e^{-z^2/2}$ decays *faster than any power* of $z$. That is mathematically
delightful — it is why every moment of the normal exists and every integral in
@sec-formal converged — and practically dangerous. A "$5$-sigma event" has probability
about $`{python} f"{p_5sigma:.1e}"`$
under the normal, i.e. roughly one in three million; many real systems (markets, network
traffic, insurance losses, material failures) produce such extremes monthly. **If your data
have heavy tails, the normal will lull you into under-pricing risk**, exactly as in
@sec-returns. The diagnostic is cheap and you should make it a reflex: plot the data against
normal quantiles (a Q–Q plot). A normal sample falls on a straight line; heavy tails bend
the ends upward.

## Skew and bounded support: the wrong shape entirely {#sec-skew}

The normal is symmetric and lives on the entire real line $(-\infty,\infty)$. Two common
data types violate this outright:

- **Inherently positive, skewed quantities** — incomes, response times, concentrations,
  rainfall — pile up near a floor of zero and trail off to the right. A normal fit puts
  positive probability on *negative* values that cannot occur, and mis-centers because mean
  $\neq$ median under skew. Reach for the **gamma, exponential, or log-normal** ([PROB-14]),
  or model $\log(\text{data})$ if a log-transform straightens the skew.
- **Counts and proportions** — a count is a non-negative integer (use Poisson, [PROB-12], or
  negative-binomial, [PROB-11]); a proportion is trapped in $[0,1]$ (use the **Beta**,
  [PROB-14]). The normal's unbounded, continuous support is simply the wrong sample space,
  even though the normal *approximation* to a binomial (de Moivre's original find) is
  serviceable when $np$ and $n(1-p)$ are both large.

```{python}
#| label: fig-failure
#| fig-cap: "Two shapes the normal cannot be. **Left:** a right-skewed positive quantity (a gamma-distributed response time); the best-fit normal (red) is symmetric, mis-centers, and absurdly assigns probability to negative times (shaded). **Right:** a Q–Q plot of the heavy-tailed returns from @sec-returns against normal quantiles — a normal sample would track the dashed line, but the tails bend sharply away, the visual fingerprint of excess kurtosis."
from scipy.stats import norm, gamma, probplot, t as student_t
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))

# skewed positive data
k, theta = 2.0, 1.3
xs = np.linspace(-3, 12, 500)
axL.plot(xs, gamma.pdf(xs, k, scale=theta), color=CURVE, label="true (gamma, skewed)")
m_g, v_g = k*theta, k*theta**2
axL.plot(xs, norm.pdf(xs, m_g, np.sqrt(v_g)), color=CURVE2, label="best-fit normal")
axL.fill_between(xs[xs<0], norm.pdf(xs[xs<0], m_g, np.sqrt(v_g)), color="#f3c0c4")
axL.set_title("Skewed, positive data"); axL.set_xlabel("response time"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

nu = 3
ret = student_t.rvs(nu, size=4000, random_state=rng)
probplot(ret, dist="norm", plot=axR)
axR.get_lines()[0].set_color(CURVE); axR.get_lines()[0].set_markersize(3)
axR.get_lines()[1].set_color(GREY); axR.get_lines()[1].set_linestyle("--")
axR.set_title("Q–Q plot: tails betray non-normality")
axR.set_xlabel("normal quantiles"); axR.set_ylabel("sample quantiles")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is assuming normality safe, and when is it dangerous?"* The summary you should be
able to recite:

- **Safe** when the quantity is a **sum or average of many independent comparable
  pieces** (the CLT regime — measurement error, sample means, aggregate sums), when a Q–Q
  plot is roughly straight, and when you care about the *center* of the distribution, not
  its extremes.
- **Dangerous** when you care about the **tails** (risk, rare-event probabilities,
  worst-case design) — thin tails systematically under-rate extremes; when the data are
  **skewed or bounded** (positive quantities, proportions, counts) — wrong shape and wrong
  support; and when the data are **heavy-tailed** (finance, network, insurance) — the variance
  may not even be a meaningful summary.
- **The deepest catch:** normality of a *statistic* (like $\bar X$, by the CLT) does not
  require normality of the *data*. Conflating the two — "I need my raw data to be normal" —
  is a classic error. Most procedures that "assume normality" actually only need the
  *sampling distribution of the estimate* to be approximately normal, which the CLT often
  supplies for free.
:::

## What the normal is *not* a limit of {#sec-not-limit}

The CLT needs a **finite variance**. If the pieces being summed have infinite variance — the
Cauchy distribution is the canonical case, with tails so heavy that $E[X^2]$ and even
$E[|X|]$ diverge (the boundary example flagged back in [PROB-5]) — then averaging does **not**
produce a normal. A sample mean of Cauchy variables is *itself* Cauchy, no matter how large
$n$ is; the averaging never tames it. This is the precise statement of the CLT's fine print:
the bell curve is the fixed point of averaging *only* when the second moment is finite. When
it is not, you are in the world of stable distributions and extreme-value theory, not the
world of this article.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the discrete catalog — [PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial, Negative-Binomial*]
and [PROB-12 *Poisson*] — and from the continuous-density groundwork of [PROB-4]. This
article built the first and most important *continuous named distribution* end to end:
its constant ([PROB-13] @sec-gaussian-integral), its moments, its standardization, and the
closure property that makes it the backbone of statistics. From here the catalog and the
vector/limit spine both continue:

- **[PROB-14 *Gamma, Exponential, and Beta*]** — the skewed and bounded distributions the
  normal *cannot* model (@sec-skew); the right tools when symmetry and full-line support
  fail.
- **[PROB-15 *Chi-Square, Student's t, and F*]** — manufactured directly out of normals: a
  sum of squared standard normals is chi-square, and the heavy-tailed $t$ of @sec-returns is
  a normal over a scaled chi-square root. Everything classical inference does with normal
  samples flows through here.
- **[PROB-17 *Random Vectors* ] and [PROB-18 *Linear Transforms*]** — the bell curve in
  many dimensions: the **multivariate normal**, where @thm-closure becomes "linear maps of a
  Gaussian vector are Gaussian," the workhorse of [REGR-6].
- **[PROB-20 *Moment Generating Functions*]** — re-proves @thm-closure in three lines using
  the normal's MGF $e^{\mu t+\sigma^2 t^2/2}$, and supplies the transform machinery the CLT
  proof needs.
- **Across the tracks:** the Gaussian error model of [REGR-2]/[REGR-9], and essentially all
  of Statistical Inference, run on the facts proved here.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Abraham de Moivre, *The Doctrine of Chances* (2nd ed., 1738), where
  the bell curve first appears as the large-$n$ binomial approximation; Carl Friedrich
  Gauss, *Theoria Motus Corporum Coelestium* (1809), which derived the curve as the unique
  error law making the arithmetic mean the most-likely estimate, welding it to least squares
  ([REGR-1]); Pierre-Simon Laplace, who connected it to general averaging (the proto-CLT).
- **The honest-tails classic.** Benoit Mandelbrot and the long literature on heavy tails in
  finance is the permanent antidote to @sec-returns; Nassim Taleb's *The Black Swan* is the
  popular sermon on the same point — the normal's thin tails as a source of real-world ruin.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why* is the normal the unique limit
  of standardized sums? — the Central Limit Theorem, proved in [INFR-A2] using the MGF/CF
  machinery of [PROB-20]. *What replaces the bell when variance is infinite?* — stable
  distributions and extreme-value theory, flagged in @sec-not-limit and [PROB-23]. *What does
  the normal look like in many correlated dimensions?* — the multivariate normal,
  [PROB-17]–[PROB-18]. *Is there a single algebraic form that contains the normal, the
  Poisson, the gamma, and the rest as special cases?* — yes, the exponential family,
  [PROB-16].
